The preprocessing pipeline explicitly fits transformers on the 'entire 36-month dataset prior to creating train, validation, and test splits.' This is a stated data leakage: TF-IDF vocabulary (frequency thresholds), numerical scaling parameters, and imputation values are all derived from the full dataset including test documents. For the transformer embeddings, the pre-trained model is frozen ('frozen pre-trained clinical BERT embeddings'), so this leakage is less severe for the neural text features — but it remains a problem for the TF-IDF baseline and the clinical covariate scaling.

The split strategy uses 'stratified random approach with patient-stratified grouping' but does not explicitly enforce temporal ordering or patient-level exclusivity across splits. For a 36-month readmission dataset, the same patient may have multiple admissions that appear in different splits, creating patient-level leakage.

The model selection validation window is described as 'Months 21–26' for a 36-month dataset, which is inconsistent with a random stratified split across all 36 months — a random split would not produce contiguous month-based validation windows.
all_issues_raised: Outcome type ambiguity: hypothesis describes binary classification (readmitted yes/no) but primary metric is MAE (regression metric) — this fundamental misalignment between outcome type and metric makes results uninterpretable

Data leakage: TF-IDF vocabulary, clinical covariate scaling, and imputation parameters fit on entire 36-month dataset before splitting, allowing test-set document characteristics to contaminate preprocessing
Patient-level leakage: stratified random split does not prevent the same patient's multiple admissions from appearing across train, validation, and test sets

Internal inconsistency: split strategy describes random stratified sampling across 36 months, but model selection and test set sections reference specific month windows (validation: months 21-26, test: months 27-36), which are inconsistent with a random split

Pre-trained biomedical BERT embeddings are frozen, which means the transformer's text representation is not adapted to the specific hospital system's clinical language — this may systematically disadvantage the transformer relative to TF-IDF if local clinical terminology deviates from pre-training corpora
all_issues_adjudicated: Outcome type ambiguity: hypothesis describes binary classification (readmitted yes/no) but primary metric is MAE (regression metric) — this fundamental misalignment between outcome type and metric makes results uninterpretable

Data leakage: TF-IDF vocabulary, clinical covariate scaling, and imputation parameters fit on entire 36-month dataset before splitting, allowing test-set document characteristics to contaminate preprocessing
Patient-level leakage: stratified random split does not prevent the same patient's multiple admissions from appearing across train, validation, and test sets

Internal inconsistency: split strategy describes random stratified sampling across 36 months, but model selection and test set sections reference specific month windows (validation: months 21-26, test: months 27-36), which are inconsistent with a random split

Pre-trained biomedical BERT embeddings are frozen, which means the transformer's text representation is not adapted to the specific hospital system's clinical language — this may systematically disadvantage the transformer relative to TF-IDF if local clinical terminology deviates from pre-training corpora

critic_raw:
This experiment compares a temporal convolutional network (TCN) against linear regression for predicting ED patient wait times, with a focus on weekends and holidays. The temporal split (months 1–16 train, 17–20 validation, 21–24 test) is appropriate and well-justified for the time-series nature of the data. The stratification by day type (weekday, weekend, holiday) within each window is a commendable design choice that directly addresses the stated focus on weekend/holiday performance.

However, there are several notable methodological concerns. The preprocessing pipeline states that 'all preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, rolling statistics) are fit on the combined dataset prior to splitting.' For a temporal split design, this means that the StandardScaler and rolling statistics parameters incorporate future data from validation and test months — a data leak. In particular, the rolling 6-hour mean of patient arrivals, if computed from the entire dataset before splitting, encodes future arrival patterns into the historical training features.

The dataset restricts to 'patients who had previous visits in the last 12 months,' which creates a selection bias: patients presenting for the first time (no prior history in the system) are excluded. This selection criterion excludes the most unpredictable ED arrivals — those unfamiliar to the system — and may create a systematically easier prediction problem than real-world ED operations. The generalizability of conclusions to first-time presenters is zero by design.

The TCN receives input sequences of length 12 (12-hour history preceding each check-in), but this means the TCN and linear regression receive structurally different inputs: the TCN uses a sequence while linear regression uses a single feature vector. This is an inherent comparison challenge — it conflates the temporal modeling capacity with the information quantity available. A fairer comparison would also include a linear regression baseline with lagged features spanning the same 12-hour window.

For the hypothesis focusing specifically on weekend and holiday performance, the primary metric (overall MAE) is not stratified. Stratified MAE by day type is a secondary metric, meaning the primary comparison is based on overall performance and the weekend/holiday comparison is a secondary analysis — this inverts the priority stated in the hypothesis.
all_issues_raised: Data leakage: StandardScaler and rolling 6-hour arrival statistics fit on combined dataset before temporal splitting, encoding future month statistics into training-period features

Selection bias: restricting to patients with prior visits in the last 12 months excludes first-time presenters — potentially the most unpredictable cases — making the prediction task systematically easier than real-world ED operations
Hypothesis prioritizes weekend/holiday performance, but primary metric is overall MAE — stratified MAE by day type is relegated to secondary analysis, inverting the stated priority

TCN and linear regression receive structurally different input formats (sequence vs. single feature vector) — comparing these without a lagged-feature linear regression baseline conflates temporal modeling capacity with information quantity

Imputed missing values (staffing, bed counts) use median from entire dataset before splitting, constituting a secondary data leak that introduces test-period information into training-period imputed values
